mapQuestions.question: Which continent is India on?
mapQuestions.answer: Asia. India sticks out like a giant triangle into the Indian Ocean 🌏

mapQuestions.question: How many people live in India?
mapQuestions.answer: About 1.45 billion. India is now the most populous country in the world, just ahead of China.

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of India?
mapQuestions.answer: New Delhi. The biggest city is Mumbai (around 21 million people in the wider area), but the seat of government is in New Delhi in the north.

mapQuestions.question: How many official languages does India have?
mapQuestions.answer: 22 official languages, plus over 1,600 other languages and dialects. Hindi and English are the two used for national government.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in India?
mapQuestions.answer: Kangchenjunga, 8,586 metres, the third-highest mountain on earth. It sits on the border with Nepal in the Himalayas.

foods.name: Biryani
foods.description: Long-grain basmati rice cooked in stages with spices, saffron, meat (lamb, chicken or goat) and crispy fried onions, then sealed under a pastry lid to steam. Hyderabad and Lucknow versions are the most famous.

foods.name: Dosa
foods.description: A massive crispy crepe made from fermented rice and lentil batter, rolled around spiced potato. From south India. Often longer than your arm. Eaten with coconut chutney and sambar.

foods.name: Samosa
foods.description: A crispy triangle of pastry stuffed with spiced potato and peas, deep-fried until golden. Eaten anywhere, any time, with sweet tamarind chutney or coriander mint chutney.

foods.name: Butter chicken
foods.description: Chicken in a creamy tomato and butter sauce mildly spiced with garam masala. Mopped up with naan bread. Invented in Delhi in the 1950s and now eaten worldwide.

foods.name: Pani puri
foods.description: Crispy hollow puris that you crack open, fill with potato, chickpeas and tangy spiced water, then pop the whole thing in your mouth. Sweet, spicy, sour, crunchy and wet in one bite.

foods.name: Masala chai
foods.description: Strong black tea boiled with milk, ginger, cardamom, cinnamon, cloves and sugar. Served in tiny clay cups at train stations, which you smash on the ground when you are done.

scavengerItems.label: A cow lying in the middle of a busy road, with no one bothering it
An auto-rickshaw (a three-wheeled yellow-and-green taxi) zipping past
A sari in a colour you have never seen on clothes before
A pile of mangoes for sale in summer (India has hundreds of varieties)
A "chaiwala" pouring tea from a great height between two cups
A small roadside shrine in a tree or wall
A street cow eating discarded vegetables (or paper)
Someone wobbling their head in a way that means yes / maybe / OK / sure
Someone in beautifully embroidered traditional clothing (anywhere, every day)
A truck painted with bright designs, bells and the words "Horn OK Please"
A temple older than your grandparents' great-grandparents
A sign in Hindi (in Devanagari script), one of dozens of scripts used in India

geoMatches.description: A capital of two cities in one: ancient Old Delhi with chaotic bazaars and Mughal mosques, and quiet tree-lined New Delhi built by the British 100 years ago.
The biggest city, on the west coast. Home of "Bollywood" film studios, Marine Drive, slums next to skyscrapers, and India's busiest train network.
The desert state in the north-west. Pink-walled Jaipur, blue-painted Jodhpur, golden Jaisalmer fortress, and camels in the Thar Desert.
Tropical state on the south-west coast. Quiet backwaters where families live on long houseboats, spice plantations, and the rainforest of the Western Ghats.
A small state on the west coast, ruled by Portugal for 450 years. Palm-fringed beaches, white-washed Portuguese churches, and India's most relaxed vibe.
The oldest continuously-inhabited city in the world (since around 1800 BC). On the Ganges river, where Hindus come to pray, bathe in the holy water, and be cremated.

senses.smell: Cardamom and cumin from a tava (flat pan), incense from a roadside shrine, marigold garlands, diesel and dust, the Ganges at dawn, rain on hot dry earth (petrichor)…
senses.hear: Car horns as language, "namaste!" and "haan-ji!", temple bells, mosque calls to prayer, the singsong of vendors, Bollywood music from every shop…
senses.taste: A dozen spices in every mouthful, sweet hot masala chai, tangy tamarind, fiery green chilli, cooling yoghurt raita, the slow burn of fresh ginger…
senses.feel: Dry desert heat (Rajasthan) or sticky monsoon rain (everywhere else), cool marble in a temple at dawn, soft cotton kurtas, the press of a Mumbai local train…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: Why are there cows wandering in the middle of the road?
stories.body: In India, cows are considered sacred by Hindus, who make up about 80% of the country. Killing a cow is illegal in most Indian states, and cows roam freely in villages, towns and even in the middle of Mumbai traffic. Many are owned by someone (a family that keeps them for milk), but they often spend the day wandering, eating discarded vegetable peelings from market stalls, and lying down wherever they like, including on motorways. Drivers swerve around them. The reasons go back thousands of years: cows give milk, dung for fuel, and once helped plough fields. In Hindu mythology, the cow represents Mother Earth, gentle, generous and never to be harmed.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your country picked one animal that no one could ever harm and it could wander anywhere it wanted, which animal should it be?

stories.question: Why did an emperor build the world's most famous tomb out of love?
stories.body: The Taj Mahal in Agra is one of the most beautiful buildings on earth. It was built between 1632 and 1653 by the Mughal emperor Shah Jahan, but not for himself, for his wife. Her name was Mumtaz Mahal, and she died giving birth to their 14th child. The emperor was so grief-stricken that he ordered the most beautiful tomb ever made. 20,000 workers and 1,000 elephants spent 22 years building it from white marble, inlaid with semi-precious stones from all over Asia. When Shah Jahan died, his son had him buried next to her, inside. The Taj changes colour depending on the time of day: pinkish in the morning, white at noon, golden at sunset, silvery in moonlight.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If you could build one beautiful thing to remember someone you love, what would it be?

stories.question: Why does India throw coloured powder at each other every spring?
stories.body: Holi is the Hindu festival of colour, held every March to celebrate the arrival of spring and the victory of good over evil. For one day, normal rules go out of the window. Everyone (and we mean EVERYONE) goes into the streets armed with bright powders called "gulal" and water guns full of coloured water, and just throws them at each other, strangers included. By the end of the day, every single person in the country is bright pink, green, yellow and blue from head to toe, and laughing. The colours come from natural sources: red from hibiscus, yellow from turmeric, green from neem leaves. Foreigners are very, very welcome to join in.
stories.thinkingQuestion: If your country had ONE day a year where the normal rules went out of the window and everyone played in the same way, what would the rule be?

stories.question: How does the world's biggest train network work?
stories.body: Indian Railways carries more than 23 MILLION passengers every single day. That is more than the entire population of Australia, riding trains in one country, every day. It is the eighth-biggest employer in the world (with 1.2 million staff). Trains range from luxurious sleeper coaches to packed unreserved carriages where people sit on the roof. They run on time mostly, although "Indian time" allows for "delays". A single ticket can take you halfway across the country for the price of a coffee. Train journeys are how most of India sees India: kids playing card games, families sharing tiffin lunches, chai sellers walking through carriages shouting "chai chai chai chai!".

stories.question: How did one man without a weapon free India from an empire?
stories.body: For nearly 200 years, India was ruled by Britain, taking its tea, cotton, spices and money. By the 1900s, Indians wanted independence. The man who led the movement was Mohandas Gandhi, called "Mahatma" (great soul). His weapon was not violence but the opposite: deliberate, peaceful refusal to follow unfair laws. He led millions of Indians on a 380km march to the sea to make salt, in protest against a British tax on it. He fasted (refused to eat) for weeks to make a point. He wore only homespun cotton. The British eventually agreed to leave, and India became independent on 15 August 1947. Gandhi was assassinated just five months later. His ideas of peaceful protest later inspired Martin Luther King Jr. in America and many others around the world.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Gandhi believed change could come without violence. Is there something you feel strongly about that you could change without fighting?

convoQuestions.question: What's the best thing you've eaten in India so far?
How spicy was the spiciest thing you have tried? Out of 10, how proud are you?
What's one thing about Indian streets that you have never seen anywhere else?
Have you found a favourite city or place yet? Why?
India is loud, colourful, full and intense. Do you find it exciting or tiring (or both)?
India has 1.4 billion people speaking dozens of different languages, all in one country. What do you think holds it all together?

wordSearch.hint: Indian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Sacred cow
animals.description: Wandering free through cities, sleeping in the middle of roads. Drivers swerve around them.

animals.name: Indian Pariah dog
animals.description: Tan-coloured street dog, the original ancient breed of South Asia.

animals.name: Rhesus macaque
animals.description: On temple walls, ancient ruins, and outside Delhi metro stations. Watch your snacks.

animals.name: Asian elephant
animals.description: In Kerala festivals, in Jaipur tourist parades, and wild in the parks of Karnataka and Kerala.

animals.name: Indian peafowl
animals.description: India's national bird. Brilliant blue, displaying tail feathers in monsoon courtship dances.

animals.name: Bengal tiger
animals.description: In Ranthambore, Bandhavgarh, Kanha. Real wild sightings are rare and unforgettable.

animals.name: Mugger crocodile
animals.description: On river banks in tiger reserves and the Chambal sanctuary.
